message.content:
I need to compare how much ranking information is extracted per provider call for these two judgment methods.

**Single Scalar Rating (A):**
- Each call produces a numeric score (e.g., 1-10) for one item on one attribute
- With N items, you need N calls to score all items
- Each call gives you an absolute position on the scale, which implicitly encodes relationship to all other items
- From N calls, you get a complete ranking of N items
- Information per call: roughly log2(N) bits (position among N items)

**Ordinal Pairwise (B):**
- Each call compares exactly 2 items and returns a binary winner
- Each call yields 1 bit of information (which of two items is better)
- To rank N items, you need O(N log N) comparisons minimum
- Information per call: 1 bit (binary choice between two items)

**Comparison:**
- Single scalar rating: Each call produces a score that can distinguish among many positions (e.g., 1-100 scale = ~6.6 bits, 1-10 scale = ~3.3 bits), and importantly, each score is comparable to all other scores, so each call contributes to the full ranking
- Pairwise: Each call yields only 1 bit of ordering information

With a 1-10 scale, single scalar gives ~3.3x more information per call than pairwise's 1 bit. With 1-100 scale, ~6.6x more. Additionally, scalar ratings allow all N items to be ranked from N calls, while pairwise needs O(N log N) calls for the same ranking.

The ratio is roughly 3-7x depending on scale granularity, but also considering that scalar ratings rank N items in N calls vs pairwise needing N log N calls, the effective advantage is larger. I'd estimate around 5-7x more information per call for scalar ratings.

```json
{"higher_id":"A","ratio":6.0,"confidence":0.75}
```
